answer:
Okay, let's try to tackle this problem. It's about Marco's friends and their Panini stickers. The goal is to show two things: first, that Marco might need at least n tables, and second, that n tables are always enough. 

Starting with part (i). The problem states that a pair of friends is "complete" if together they have all n stickers. Marco doesn't want any complete pair at the same table. So, he needs to seat his friends in such a way that no two friends at the same table form a complete pair. The question is to show that in some cases, he might need as many as n tables. 

Hmm. So, maybe we need to construct an example where each friend's collection is missing exactly one sticker, and each sticker is missing by exactly one friend. That way, any two friends would together have all stickers except maybe one. Wait, no. If each friend is missing a different sticker, then any two friends would together have all stickers except possibly two. Wait, let me think. Suppose there are n friends, each missing a unique sticker. So friend 1 is missing sticker 1, friend 2 missing sticker 2, etc. Then, if two friends are at the same table, say friend i and friend j, their combined collection would have all stickers except sticker i and sticker j. So they are missing two stickers, so they are not complete. Therefore, in this case, any two friends at the same table would not form a complete pair. Wait, but then why would Marco need n tables? Because if all friends can be seated together without any complete pair, then one table would suffice. But that contradicts the idea that n tables might be needed. 

Wait, maybe I misunderstood the problem. Let me re-read. The problem says that a pair is complete if their joint collection has at least one of each sticker. So, if two friends together have all n stickers, they form a complete pair. Marco wants to avoid having any such pair at the same table. 

So, in the example where each friend is missing a unique sticker, then any two friends would together have n-2 stickers missing. Wait, no. If friend A is missing sticker 1, and friend B is missing sticker 2, then together they have all stickers except 1 and 2. So they are missing two stickers, so they are not complete. Therefore, in this case, even if all friends are seated at the same table, there are no complete pairs. So Marco could seat everyone at one table. But that would mean that part (i) is not possible. So my initial thought is wrong. 

Therefore, maybe the example needs to be different. Maybe we need a scenario where every pair of friends forms a complete pair. Then, Marco would have to seat each friend at a separate table. But that would require n tables. But is that possible? 

Wait, if every pair of friends together has all n stickers, then each friend must be missing exactly one sticker, and each sticker is missing by exactly one friend. Wait, but if there are n friends, each missing a different sticker, then as before, any two friends would be missing two stickers. So that's not the case. 

Alternatively, maybe each friend is missing a different set of stickers. For example, suppose that for each sticker, there is exactly one friend who has it. Wait, no. If each friend is missing a unique sticker, then each sticker is present in n-1 friends. So, for any two friends, they are missing two different stickers. Therefore, their combined collection is missing two stickers. So they are not complete. 

So in that case, even if all friends are at the same table, there are no complete pairs. Therefore, Marco could seat everyone at one table. But that would mean that part (i) is not possible. So maybe my approach is wrong. 

Alternatively, maybe the problem is that Marco's friends have collections such that every pair of friends is complete. Then, Marco would need to seat each friend at a separate table. But is that possible? 

Wait, if every pair of friends is complete, that would mean that for any two friends, their combined collection has all n stickers. So, for each sticker, at least one of the two friends has it. But if that's true for every pair, then each sticker must be present in all but at most one friend. Because if a sticker is missing from two friends, then those two friends would form a pair that doesn't have that sticker. 

So, for example, if there are n friends, and each sticker is missing from exactly one friend. Then, for any two friends, since each is missing a different sticker, their combined collection would have all stickers except two. Wait, no. If each sticker is missing from exactly one friend, then for any two friends, they are missing two different stickers. Therefore, their combined collection is missing two stickers. So they are not complete. 

Therefore, in that case, even if all friends are at the same table, there are no complete pairs. So Marco could seat everyone at one table. 

But the problem says that Marco might need to reserve at least n tables. So there must be a scenario where Marco cannot seat more than one friend per table. That is, every pair of friends forms a complete pair. 

But how can that happen? For every pair of friends, their combined collection has all n stickers. That would require that for every pair of friends, every sticker is present in at least one of them. 

So, for each sticker, it must be present in all but at most one friend. Because if a sticker is missing from two friends, then those two friends would form a pair that doesn't have that sticker. 

Therefore, if each sticker is missing from exactly one friend, then for any two friends, they are missing two different stickers. So their combined collection is missing two stickers. Therefore, they are not complete. 

But if we want that every pair of friends is complete, then each sticker must be present in all but at most zero friends. That is, every sticker is present in all friends. But then, any pair would have all stickers. But that's trivial. 

Alternatively, maybe each sticker is missing from exactly one friend, but arranged in such a way that for any two friends, they are missing different stickers. Wait, but that's the same as before. 

Wait, maybe if each friend is missing a unique sticker, but there are n friends and n stickers. So each friend is missing one unique sticker. Then, for any two friends, they are missing two different stickers. Therefore, their combined collection is missing two stickers. So they are not complete. 

Therefore, in this case, Marco can seat all friends at one table. 

But the problem says that Marco might need to reserve at least n tables. So there must be a case where Marco cannot seat more than one friend per table. That is, every pair of friends is complete. 

But how can that happen? For every pair of friends, their combined collection has all n stickers. 

This would require that for every pair of friends, every sticker is present in at least one of them. 

So, for each sticker, it must be present in all but at most one friend. Because if a sticker is missing from two friends, then those two friends would form a pair that doesn't have that sticker. 

Therefore, if each sticker is missing from exactly one friend, then for any two friends, they are missing two different stickers. So their combined collection is missing two stickers. Therefore, they are not complete. 

But if we want that every pair is complete, then each sticker must be present in all but zero friends. That is, every sticker is present in all friends. But then, any pair would have all stickers. 

But that's trivial. So in that case, Marco would need to seat each friend at a separate table. But that's only if every pair is complete. 

But how can we have a situation where every pair is complete? 

Suppose that each friend has all stickers except one, and each sticker is missing from exactly one friend. Then, as before, any two friends are missing two stickers. So they are not complete. 

Alternatively, suppose that each friend is missing a different set of stickers. For example, friend 1 is missing sticker 1, friend 2 is missing stickers 1 and 2, etc. But that might not work. 

Alternatively, maybe each friend is missing a unique sticker, but there are more friends than stickers. Wait, but the problem states there are n stickers. 

Wait, maybe the key is that if each friend is missing a different sticker, but there are n friends. So each friend is missing one unique sticker. Then, as before, any two friends are missing two stickers. So they are not complete. 

But if we have n friends, each missing a different sticker, then for any two friends, they are missing two stickers. So they are not complete. Therefore, Marco can seat all friends at one table. 

But the problem says that Marco might need to reserve at least n tables. So there must be a case where Marco cannot seat more than one friend per table. 

Wait, maybe if each friend's collection is such that for any two friends, their combined collection is complete. That is, every pair of friends is complete. Then, Marco would have to seat each friend at a separate table. 

But how can that happen? 

For every pair of friends, their combined collection has all n stickers. 

This would require that for each sticker, it is present in all but at most one friend. Because if a sticker is missing from two friends, then those two friends would form a pair that doesn't have that sticker. 

Therefore, if each sticker is missing from exactly one friend, then for any two friends, they are missing two different stickers. So their combined collection is missing two stickers. Therefore, they are not complete. 

But if we want that every pair is complete, then each sticker must be present in all but zero friends. That is, every sticker is present in all friends. 

But that's trivial. 

Alternatively, maybe each sticker is missing from exactly one friend, but there are n friends and n stickers. So each friend is missing one unique sticker. Then, as before, any two friends are missing two stickers. 

So, in that case, no pair is complete. 

Therefore, to have every pair complete, we need that for every pair of friends, every sticker is present in at least one of them. 

Which would require that for each sticker, it is present in all but at most one friend. 

But if we have n friends, and for each sticker, it is missing from exactly one friend, then for any two friends, they are missing two different stickers. 

So, to have every pair complete, we need that for each sticker, it is missing from at most one friend. 

But if we have n friends, and n stickers, and each sticker is missing from exactly one friend, then for any two friends, they are missing two stickers. 

Therefore, to have every pair complete, we need that for each sticker, it is missing from zero friends. That is, every sticker is present in all friends. 

But that's trivial. 

Alternatively, maybe there's a different configuration. 

Wait, maybe if each friend is missing a different set of stickers, but arranged such that any two friends together have all stickers. 

For example, suppose n=2. Then, if there are two friends. If each friend is missing one sticker, but different ones. Then, together they have both stickers. So they form a complete pair. Therefore, Marco would need to seat them at separate tables. 

But for n=2, that would require 2 tables. 

Similarly, for n=3. Suppose each friend is missing a different sticker. Then, any two friends would be missing two stickers. So they are not complete. Therefore, Marco can seat all three at one table. 

But if we want that every pair is complete, then for n=3, each pair must have all three stickers. 

So, for each pair of friends, their combined collection has all three stickers. 

This would require that for each sticker, it is present in at least two friends. Because if a sticker is missing from two friends, then those two friends would form a pair that doesn't have that sticker. 

So, for n=3, if each sticker is present in at least two friends, then any pair of friends would have all stickers. 

For example, suppose each sticker is present in two friends. Then, for any pair of friends, since each sticker is present in two friends, at least one of the two friends has it. 

Wait, no. If a sticker is present in two friends, then there is one friend who doesn't have it. So if we take a pair that includes that friend and another, then the other friend has the sticker. 

Wait, let's take an example. Suppose n=3 stickers: A, B, C. 

Friend 1 has A and B. 

Friend 2 has A and C. 

Friend 3 has B and C. 

Now, any pair of friends: 

Friend 1 and 2: A, B, C. 

Friend 1 and 3: A, B, C. 

Friend 2 and 3: A, B, C. 

So every pair is complete. 

In this case, Marco would need to seat each friend at a separate table, because any two friends form a complete pair. 

Therefore, for n=3, Marco might need 3 tables. 

Similarly, for general n, if we can arrange the collections such that every pair of friends is complete, then Marco would need n tables. 

So, how to construct such a scenario? 

For each sticker, it must be present in at least n-1 friends. Because if a sticker is missing from two friends, then those two friends would form a pair that doesn't have that sticker. 

Therefore, to ensure that every pair of friends is complete, each sticker must be present in all but at most one friend. 

So, if we have n friends and n stickers, and for each sticker, it is missing from exactly one friend. Then, for any two friends, they are missing two different stickers. Wait, no. If each sticker is missing from exactly one friend, then for any two friends, they are missing two different stickers. Therefore, their combined collection is missing two stickers. So they are not complete. 

But in the previous example with n=3, each sticker is missing from one friend. For example, sticker A is missing from friend 3, sticker B from friend 2, sticker C from friend 1. Then, any two friends would be missing two stickers. 

Wait, but in the example I gave earlier, each sticker is present in two friends. So for n=3, each sticker is present in two friends, missing from one. 

But in that case, any two friends would have all stickers. 

Wait, let's check. 

Friend 1: A, B 

Friend 2: A, C 

Friend 3: B, C 

Each sticker is present in two friends. 

Now, take any two friends. 

Friend 1 and 2: A, B, C. 

Friend 1 and 3: A, B, C. 

Friend 2 and 3: A, B, C. 

Yes, so every pair is complete. 

But in this case, each sticker is missing from one friend. 

So, for general n, if we can arrange that each sticker is missing from exactly one friend, and each friend is missing exactly one sticker, then for any two friends, they are missing two different stickers. Wait, no. If each friend is missing one sticker, and each sticker is missing from one friend, then for any two friends, they are missing two different stickers. 

But in the n=3 case, that's not true. Because in the example, each friend is missing one sticker, but any two friends together have all stickers. 

Wait, there's a contradiction here. 

Wait, in the n=3 example, each friend is missing one sticker. Friend 1 is missing C, friend 2 is missing B, friend 3 is missing A. 

So, friend 1 and 2 together have A, B, C. 

Friend 1 and 3 together have A, B, C. 

Friend 2 and 3 together have A, B, C. 

So even though each is missing one sticker, any two together have all stickers. 

Ah, I see. So in this case, even though each friend is missing one sticker, any two friends together have all stickers. 

So, the key is that for each sticker, it is missing from exactly one friend. Therefore, for any two friends, since each is missing a different sticker, their combined collection includes all stickers except those two. Wait, no. 

Wait, in the n=3 example, each friend is missing one sticker. So friend 1 is missing C, friend 2 missing B, friend 3 missing A. 

So, friend 1 and 2 together have A, B, and C? Wait, friend 1 has A and B, friend 2 has A and C. So together, they have A, B, C. 

Similarly, friend 1 and 3: friend 1 has A and B, friend 3 has B and C. Together, A, B, C. 

Friend 2 and 3: friend 2 has A and C, friend 3 has B and C. Together, A, B, C. 

So even though each is missing one sticker, any two together have all stickers. 

So, in this case, every pair is complete. 

Therefore, for general n, if we can arrange that each sticker is missing from exactly one friend, and each friend is missing exactly one sticker, then any two friends together have all stickers.
